front: Musical: The protagonist is a Black queer usher working at a production of The Lion King while trying to write an autobiographical musical — which is the musical itself
Musical: The protagonist's mother calls on his birthday to 'remind him periodically' that she loves him, then tells him 'hell is real'
Musical: A song in this show describes how 'white girls can do anything, can't they / black boys must always obey their mothers'
Musical: In this show, Tyler Perry seeks a ghostwriter for a gospel play; the protagonist is then accused of being a race traitor
Musical: The protagonist battles personified 'Thoughts' including his 'daily self-loathing' and a white 'Inwood Daddy'
Song: An ostinato on 'Usher Usher' opens this number, in which the protagonist asks 'how many minutes 'til the end of intermission'
Song: In this parody number, a character acts out three characters discussing Michelle's inability to find a husband
back: "Show Me How to Pray" (A Strange Loop)

front: Musical: A preacher sermonizes that 'the only thing worse than dying of AIDS would be living with it' after recalling cousin Darnell's refusal of HIV medication
Musical: This show's title comes from a Douglas Hofstadter concept about self-referential systems; it won the 2022 Tony for Best Musical and the 2020 Pulitzer Prize for Drama
Musical: Michael R. Jackson also wrote this other recent musical besides A Strange Loop
Musical: In this show, the protagonist receives a voicemail from his dad containing Scott Rudin's phone number, while working at a production of The Lion King
Playwright: Besides A Strange Loop and White Girl in Danger, this playwright wrote a 2024 non-musical play adapting the film Teeth
